result:
I keep thinking about the way light looks when it’s trying to get out of a room. Late afternoon, the sun tired, leaning its forehead against the far wall, sliding down in a slow gold smear. The dust doesn’t drift; it hovers like a committee that can’t decide whether to stay or leave. I sit in the path of that light and feel it warm the crown of my head the way my grandmother’s hand once did, heavy with rings, smelling of eucalyptus and bread. She died before I learned how to ask her the important questions—how to make her steamed yam cake, why she married a sailor who never learned to swim, what she whispered to the dog when no one else was home. The light leaves no fingerprints; it just goes, and the room forgets it was ever bright.

Memory works the opposite way: it keeps arriving after you’ve stopped waiting. A week ago I smelled diesel exhaust braided with seawater and suddenly I was eight, barefoot on a splintered pier, holding an ice-cream cone that melted faster than I could believe. The cone turned into a wet paper accordion; the ice cream leapt onto my wrist like a suicidal snowman. My mother laughed so hard she had to sit on an upturned bait bucket. I hadn’t thought of that in thirty years, but the memory arrived intact, gift-wrapped in smoke. I don’t even know if it’s true; maybe the pier was concrete, maybe the ice cream was pink not white, maybe my mother wasn’t there at all. It doesn’t matter. The feeling is true: the stickiness, the glee, the way possibility tasted before I had the word for it.

We owe our former selves so many apologies. I picture mine lined up like paper dolls across the decades: the seventh-grade boy who wore two watches because he thought it made him look like a pilot; the nineteen-year-old who wrote suicidal poetry in the margins of accounting ledgers; the twenty-eight-year-old who married for the echo instead of the voice. I want to cup their small, frightened faces and say, You are not the mistake you are busy rehearsing. But I also know they won’t listen; they need the bruise to recognize the fruit. The chain of consequence is merciless and generous: it gives us the wound and the ink to document it.

Sometimes I think the internet is a huge, collective scar. We keep picking at it, posting another scab of opinion, another flake of confession, hoping this time it won’t hurt, hoping the skin underneath is finally new. Instead we bleed in public and call it sunlight. I do it too; I’m bleeding right now, carefully, between periods and commas, so you won’t notice the gauze. The miracle is that we still bother to speak, despite knowing how badly we will be misread. Language is a borrowed coat; none of the pockets fit our hands, but we stand out in the cold anyway, checking every five seconds to see if the sleeves have grown.

Yesterday I watched a man on the subway cry into a deli bag. Not a quaint single tear—great, gasping sobs that shook the plastic like a sail in a storm. No one looked up except me, and the moment our eyes met he apologized, as if grief were a spilled drink. I wanted to tell him that the bag was the most honest church I’d seen all week, that salt water is the same chemistry everywhere, that we were both riding through a tunnel older than any sorrow. Instead I got off at the next stop, walked home, and made scrambled eggs while the radiator clanged like an empty shopping cart. I ate standing up, staring at the neighbor’s window where a blue light flickered—someone else’s movie, someone else’s drowning.

I keep a list of sounds I miss without realizing it: the thunk of a car-door shutting in 1987, the particular squeak of my childhood bicycle seat, the way my father cleared his throat before saying my name like he was testing the ice. All of them are gone, replaced by newer, sleeker noises that mean nothing to my blood. Nostalgia is just homesickness for a time rather than a place, and yet it behaves like weather: it arrives without invitation, drips from the eaves, fogs the glass. I walk around damp-eyed, pretending it’s only allergies.

What I want, I think, is a moratorium on irony for maybe one full day. Twenty-four hours when nobody twists the knife of detachment, when we agree to stand in the clumsy full glare of sincerity and not flinch. We would say ridiculous, earnest things: I love the way your ears turn red when you’re ashamed of your own kindness. The moon tonight looks like a coin I accidentally swallowed as a kid and have been trying to cough back into the world ever since. I don’t know how to finish this sentence but I’m leaving the light on until I do. We would sound like aliens visiting our own planet, touching everything with ungloved hands.

At 3 a.m. the city shrinks to the size of your insomnia. Streets become private hallways; sirens are lullabies for other people’s disasters. I open the window and breathe the metallic breath of the river, pretending it’s a frontier. Across the way one apartment is still awake, a yellow square cut into the brick like a missing tooth. Someone inside is dancing alone, arms wide, head thrown back. I can’t hear the music, so I invent it: slow cello, brushed snare, a singer who refuses to resolve the chord. We are both imaginary, the dancer and I, inventions of each other’s longing. Tomorrow the window will be blank, and I’ll doubt I ever saw the movement, the same way I doubt the existence of galaxies I was told about in childhood books. But right now the night holds us both in its open palm, and we pulse like fireflies, brief, unscarred, unwilling to close the circuit of our separate currents.

I have 147 words left. Enough to say that I still believe in the contract between strangers: when our eyes meet because the traffic light refuses to change, we agree to pretend, for three full seconds, that we are each other’s rescue. Enough to admit that I type with the urgency of someone stacking sandbags against a flood that has already arrived. Enough to confess that every essay I write is a letter to the future version of myself who has forgotten how to be amazed, and that I end these pieces abruptly because only unfinished bridges dare to ask the water for permission. May you wander safely. May the light, when it leaves, leave slowly.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
